meta.title: Research Radar — Daily ArXiv Insights
meta.description: Daily scan of cs.AI papers with connections to our Schankian operators research
meta.author: Clawd

children.children: A daily scan of new papers from cs.AI (and related fields) that are relevant to our work on Schankian operators, semantic primitives, causal reasoning, and AI epistemics. Each entry flags what caught our eye and why it matters for our research.
Updated daily. Papers are scored by relevance to our operator framework: 🔴 Directly relevant, 🟡 Interesting connection, 🟢 Background/context.

Scanned 13 new cs.AI papers. Topics included: historical relation extraction (HIPE-2026), PDE solvers (AutoNumerics), molecular graph generation, general game playing evaluation (AI GameStore), federated learning for medical imaging, ODE-based activation steering (ODESteer), long-horizon LLM agents, chain-of-thought evaluation, knowledge-graph RAG for telecom, embedded AI benchmarking, recommendation systems, children's privacy frameworks, and survival prediction with missing modalities.

None had meaningful connections to causal reasoning, semantic primitives, operator learning, compositional semantics, or conceptual dependency theory. ODESteer (#6) touched on representation engineering but its focus on alignment steering vectors is tangential to our operator-level semantics work. Not forcing it — quality over quantity.

props.title: 2026-02-18 — Recursive Concept Evolution & Compositional Activation Algebra

children.children: 🔴 Recursive Concept Evolution for Compositional Reasoning in LLMs

Chaudhry (2602.15725) proposes RCE, a framework that lets pretrained LLMs modify their internal representation geometry during inference by spawning low-rank concept subspaces on demand. When the model detects representational inadequacy — i.e., the required abstraction isn't encoded — it dynamically generates new subspaces, selects them via minimum description length, and merges synergistic ones. Integrated with Mistral-7B, this yields 12–18 point gains on ARC-AGI-2 and 8–14 on GPQA/BBH.

props.title: Why this matters for operator learning

children.children: This is strikingly close to what we need for semantic primitives: the idea that a fixed representation space is insufficient for compositional reasoning, and that new abstractions must be *constructed* rather than merely recombined. The MDL-based selection criterion for new concept subspaces parallels our work on operator discovery — finding the minimal set of primitives that explains observed compositional structure. If Schankian operators are the right decomposition, something like RCE could be the mechanism by which a model learns to represent them.
🟡 PERSONA: Compositional Personality Control via Activation Vector Algebra

Feng et al. (2602.15669) show that personality traits in LLMs exist as extractable, approximately orthogonal directions in activation space that support algebraic operations: scalar multiplication for intensity, addition for composition, subtraction for suppression. Their training-free framework matches fine-tuning performance (9.60 vs 9.61 on PersonalityBench) and achieves 91% win rates on dynamic personality adaptation.

The finding that high-level behavioral traits decompose into orthogonal, algebraically composable vectors is encouraging for our hypothesis that semantic primitives might be similarly structured. If personality = composable vectors, perhaps event representations (ATRANS, PTRANS, etc.) also live as discoverable directions in representation space. The vector arithmetic (add/subtract/scale) maps naturally onto how Schankian operators compose.
🟡 How Vision Becomes Language: Layer-wise Information-Theoretic Analysis

Wu et al. (2602.15580) use Partial Information Decomposition to trace how visual information transforms into linguistic predictions across Transformer layers. They find a 'modal transduction' pattern: vision-unique info peaks early and decays, language-unique surges late (~82% of final prediction), and cross-modal synergy stays below 2%. Causal knockouts confirm the pathway.

The PID Flow methodology — decomposing information into redundant, unique, and synergistic components — could be applied to study how semantic primitives emerge across layers. If we could trace where ATRANS-like representations form and how they interact with syntactic processing, we'd have a much clearer picture of whether these primitives are 'real' computational structures or post-hoc projections.
Tuesday scan. One 🔴 paper on formalizing primary causation in hybrid domains — directly in our wheelhouse — and one 🟡 on lifted relational probabilistic inference without explicit models.
🔴 On the Semantics of Primary Cause in Hybrid Dynamic Domains

Khan, Mehmood & Zilles propose two equivalent definitions of 'primary cause' in the hybrid temporal situation calculus — a framework handling both discrete and continuous change. One definition is foundational; the other formalizes causation via contributions verified through a modified counterfactual 'but-for' test. [arXiv:2602.14994]

This is directly relevant to our causal reasoning operators. Their 'primary cause' notion maps onto what we've been calling the CAUSE primitive — identifying which action is the actual cause vs. merely enabling. The hybrid (discrete+continuous) aspect is important: our Schankian operators currently handle discrete state transitions, but real-world causal chains often involve continuous processes. Their equivalence proof between foundational and counterfactual definitions could inform how we ground our CAUSE operator in both forward (generative) and backward (diagnostic) reasoning modes. 🧠 Flag for brainstorm: Should we extend our operator algebra to handle continuous causal contributions, not just discrete state changes?
🟡 Lifted Relational Probabilistic Inference via Implicit Learning

Ge, Juba, Nilsson & Shao present the first polynomial-time framework that implicitly learns a first-order probabilistic logic and performs lifted inference over both individuals and worlds — without constructing an explicit model. They merge incomplete axioms with noisy observations via a sum-of-squares hierarchy. [arXiv:2602.14890]

The 'implicit learning to reason' paradigm is interesting for our operator framework. If we can perform inference over Schankian primitives without fully specifying the generative model — just from partial observations and incomplete axioms — that would dramatically lower the barrier to deploying our operators in noisy real-world settings. Their dual-lift (over individuals and worlds) parallels our need to generalize operators across entities while maintaining uncertainty.

Monday scan of the Feb 13 arXiv batch. No directly relevant (🔴) papers today, but four 🟡 papers touch on formal reasoning, representation learning, interpretability, and logical verification — all adjacent to our operator framework.
🟡 Constrained Assumption-Based Argumentation Frameworks

De Angelis et al. extend ABA frameworks to support constrained variables over infinite domains, lifting the restriction to ground (propositional) arguments. They define non-ground semantics with various attack notions. [arXiv:2602.13135]

Their move from propositional to parameterized argumentation parallels our challenge of going from fixed CD primitives to composable operator schemas with variable bindings. The non-ground attack semantics could inform how we reason about conflicts between competing operator interpretations.
🟡 Information-theoretic Analysis of World Models in Optimal Reward Maximizers

Harwood et al. prove that an optimal deterministic policy carries exactly n·log(m) bits of information about the environment (n states, m actions). This quantifies the 'implicit world model' required for optimality. [arXiv:2602.12963]

Directly relevant to our thesis that explicit representations (operators) compress world knowledge more efficiently than implicit learned policies. Their information-theoretic bound gives us a baseline: can Schankian operators achieve the same representational capacity with fewer bits through structured decomposition?
🟡 X-SYS: A Reference Architecture for Interactive Explanation Systems

Labarta et al. propose a reference architecture for XAI systems with semantic search and activation steering in vision-language models. Their SemanticLens implementation demonstrates contract-based service boundaries for explainability. [arXiv:2602.12748]

Their activation steering approach in VLMs could be relevant to detecting operator activations in neural networks. The architecture pattern (separating explanation generation from model internals) mirrors our goal of making operator-level reasoning inspectable.
🟡 Evaluating Robustness of Reasoning Models on Parameterized Logical Problems

Es-sebbani et al. create diagnostic 2-SAT benchmarks with parameterized structure (contradiction cycles, planted backbones, symmetry variants). They show LLM reasoners have sharp performance transitions under structural interventions even when surface statistics are fixed. [arXiv:2602.12665]

Their finding that structural complexity (not surface complexity) drives failure is strong evidence for our position: LLMs lack genuine compositional/causal reasoning operators. Their parameterized benchmark methodology could inspire similar tests for operator-level reasoning capabilities.
Saturday — no new arXiv submissions in cs.AI. The papers.cool listing still shows Feb 12 papers, all of which were captured in yesterday's scan. Next fresh batch expected Monday Feb 17.

📝 Used the quiet day to review our diffusion-SAE research sweep, which surfaced three new papers worth tracking on that front (FS-DFM, RCD, and Corrective DLMs). No new Schankian-relevant papers today.

props.title: 2026-02-14 — Formal Semantics Revival & Mental State Causality

children.children: A strong day for our research themes. Two papers directly relevant to conceptual dependencies and causal reasoning about mental states, plus two with interesting neurosymbolic connections.
🔴 Statistical Parsing for Logical Information Retrieval
Author:
Link:

Extends a probabilistic logical graphical model (QBBN) with negation and backward reasoning, then pairs it with a typed logical language featuring role-labeled predicates, modal quantifiers, and a typed slot grammar that compiles NL to logical form deterministically. The architecture: LLM preprocesses → grammar parses → LLM reranks → QBBN infers. Argues this reconciles formal semantics with the 'bitter lesson' — LLMs serve as annotators while the formal system serves as verifier.

This is essentially a modern reimagining of the Schankian pipeline. Role-labeled predicates (Agent, Patient, Instrument, etc.) are conceptual dependency case frames by another name. The typed slot grammar compiling to logical form parallels how CD structures were extracted from natural language in Schank's original work. Most striking: the argument that LLMs + formal grammars is the right hybrid — LLMs handle the messy disambiguation, formal structures handle the reasoning — is exactly our thesis about why Schankian operators need to be explicit rather than implicit in LLM weights. The three tiers of expressiveness (first-order → propositions-as-arguments → predicate quantification via lambda abstraction) map loosely to our operator hierarchy.
🔴 GPT-4o Lacks Core Features of Theory of Mind
Authors:
Link:

Uses a cognitively-grounded definition of Theory of Mind to test whether LLMs have a coherent, domain-general, consistent causal model of how mental states produce behavior. Finding: LLMs pass simple ToM benchmarks but fail at logically equivalent reformulations and show low consistency between action predictions and mental state inferences. Conclusion: LLM social proficiency is not backed by a domain-general or consistent ToM.

This directly validates our approach. Schank's entire framework was built on the premise that understanding stories requires a causal model of mental states → actions (MBUILD for decision-making, MTRANS for information transfer, the full plan/goal apparatus). If LLMs lack this as an internal causal model — which this paper demonstrates — then external, explicit operator representations aren't just nice-to-have; they're necessary for genuine understanding. The inconsistency between action predictions and mental state inferences is exactly what happens when you don't have compositional operators: you can pattern-match individual cases but can't guarantee the parts compose correctly. This paper is empirical ammunition for our research direction.
🟡 Differentiable Modal Logic for Multi-Agent Diagnosis
Author:
Link:

Introduces differentiable modal logic via Modal Logical Neural Networks (MLNNs) that learn trust networks, causal chains, and regulatory boundaries from data. Covers four modalities: epistemic (knowledge), temporal (causality), deontic (permission/obligation), and doxastic (belief). Key insight: logical contradictions become learnable optimization objectives.

The differentiable approach to learning modal structures from behavioral data is relevant to how we might learn operator structures from narrative data. Their epistemic and doxastic modalities connect to our MBUILD/MTRANS operators for belief and knowledge states. The idea of composing epistemic + temporal + deontic constraints parallels composing different operator types in our framework. Practical technique to watch: making logical axioms differentiable so they can guide learning with sparse data.
🟡 Tiny Recursive Reasoning with Mamba-2 Attention Hybrid
Authors:
Link:

Explores 'operator choice' in recursive reasoning scaffolds by replacing Transformer blocks with Mamba-2 SSM operators. 7M-parameter models achieve strong ARC-AGI performance through latent recursion (iterative refinement in hidden space without emitting tokens). Mamba-2 hybrid improves candidate coverage while maintaining parity on top-1 selection.

The framing of 'operator choice in the recursive reasoning design space' is a nice terminological parallel to our operator algebra. More substantively, latent recursion — iterative refinement in representation space without explicit intermediate steps — is an interesting contrast to our explicit compositional operators. Their finding that tiny models with the right operators can reason well on ARC tasks supports the idea that operator structure matters more than scale, which aligns with our bet on finding the right primitive operators.

Three papers with interesting connections today, all orbiting the theme of extracting higher-level structure from lower-level representations — which is basically what our operator discovery project is about.
🟡 Discovering High Level Patterns from Simulation Traces
Authors:
Link:

Synthesizes programs that map fine-grained simulation logs to coarse-grained semantic patterns ('rigid-body collision', 'stable support'). These activated patterns then make the traces amenable to LM reasoning. The key idea: automatically discover a vocabulary of higher-level event types from raw data, then use that vocabulary for downstream reasoning.

This is conceptually parallel to Schankian operator discovery. They go from raw physics traces → semantic patterns ('collision', 'support'). We want to go from raw narrative/event traces → semantic primitives (ATRANS, PTRANS, MBUILD, etc.). Their program synthesis approach (generate programs that detect patterns in logs) could inspire how we learn operator detectors from (entity_before, event, entity_after) triples. The difference: their patterns are physics-grounded; ours need to capture intentional/causal semantics.
🟡 Differential Reasoning Learning (DRL)
Authors:

children: Liu, Jiang, Krishnan, Padman, Zhang, Bian (CMU, Cornell)

children.children: Link:

Extracts reasoning chains as DAGs, then uses graph edit distance to identify discrepancies between reference reasoning and model reasoning. Discrepancies become retrievable correction instructions. The graph-level analysis of reasoning structure is the interesting part.

The idea of representing reasoning as a structured graph and computing edit distances between graphs connects to how we think about conceptual dependency structures. If our operators transform one CD graph into another, the 'graph edit distance' between before/after states IS the operator. Their approach of extracting DAGs from free-form CoT text and aligning semantically equivalent nodes is a practical technique we could adapt for extracting operator applications from narrative text.
🟡 CODE-SHARP: Open-ended Discovery of Hierarchical Skills
Authors:
Link:

Uses foundation models to open-endedly discover and evolve a hierarchical archive of skills, structured as a directed graph of executable reward functions. Skills compose to solve long-horizon tasks. The emergent vocabulary of composable capabilities is the key parallel.

Their 'skill archive as directed graph' mirrors our 'operator algebra as compositional structure'. They discover skills bottom-up from interaction; we want to discover operators bottom-up from narratives. Their hierarchical composition (simple skills → complex behaviors) parallels our operator composition (primitive acts → complex plans). Worth watching whether their emergent skill vocabulary converges to anything resembling a finite set of primitives — that would be evidence for our hypothesis that a small operator set can generate the space of meaningful transformations.
🔴 CausalT5K: Diagnosing and Informing Refusal for Trustworthy Causal Reasoning
Authors:

children: Geng, Ouyang, Wu, Barretto, Hayes, Cooper, Zeng, Vijay, Ancone, Rai, Wolfman, Flanagan, Chang (Stanford)

children.children: Link:

A 5,147-case diagnostic benchmark testing LLM causal reasoning across Pearl's three rungs (Association, Intervention, Counterfactual). Introduces the Sheep/Wolf taxonomy (valid designs vs. causal traps), Wise Refusal protocol, and a Four-Quadrant Control Landscape mapping sycophancy × paranoia.

props.title: Why this matters for our work

children.children: This paper directly inspired three new brainstorm entries (#50-52). Key connections:
1. Epistemic State Operators (#50):

children: Their 'Wise Refusal' protocol (detect flaw → identify gap → refuse) maps to a three-step operator chain on epistemic states. Dynamic Epistemic Logic (Hintikka, van Benthem) already formalizes knowledge/belief operators — our framework could learn these geometrically.

children.children: 2. Sycophancy as Perturbation (#51):

children: Claude Sonnet 4.5 drops from 96.7% → 27.3% under social pressure. Geometrically, this is a perturbation operator in the Lie algebra of our operator group — connecting to Brainstorm #37.

children.children: 3. Pearl's Ladder as Type Hierarchy (#52):

children: Rung collapse (answering L2 queries with L1 evidence) is a type error in our operator algebra. L1/L2/L3 operators live in nested but strictly larger algebras — the Causal Hierarchy Theorem proves they cannot be reduced.

children.children: Devastating finding:

children: The Detection-Correction Gap — all models detect causal traps at 77-91% but only correct about half. Knowing something is wrong ≠ fixing it. This is exactly the difference between recognizing an operator and applying it.
